text: The reaction of uranium monocarbide with molten metal chlorides $\left(\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}, \mathrm{ZnCl}_{2}, \mathrm{CdCl}_{2}, \mathrm{HgCl}_{2}, \mathrm{MnCl}_{2}\right)$ in molten alkali chlorides (NaCl, KCl, RbCl, CsCl and eutectics NaCl/KCl, NaCl/CaCl $_2$ ) in temperature region between 600 to $970^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in sealed silica ampules was investigated.

The aim of this paper is to discuss some particular problems concerning the kinetics and equilibrium of the reaction between uranium monocarbide and transient metal chlorides in molten chloride media in temperature range up to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The reason for such a study is: (1).
The development of uranium-plutonium carbide as a solid fuel for fast breeder reactors (e.g. see Schumacher 1971).
(2) The possibility that pyrochemical processes might be employed with molten chloride media for reprocessing and preparation of uranium carbide fuel (Vogler, Argonne Nat.
1965, Beucherie 1966, Taube, Schumacher 1970).

(3) The investigation of the properties of uranium trichloride in molten chloride media for possible use as fuel or blanket/coolant material in a molten salt fast breeder reactor (Taube, Ligou 1972) (see also Harder, Long 1969).

The most intensive investigation of the chlorination of uranium (or thorium) carbide in molten salt media was carried out using a strong chlorination agent (Ishihara 1964) or electrolysis (Hansen 1963).
It is that of Robinson and Chiotti (1964) in which a system of uranium monocarbide in molten metallic zinc media is equilibrated with molten zinc chloride in KCl/LiCl media.

The uranium monocarbide used is almost always a uranium oxycarbide which influences reaction 1) in the following simplified manner:

The main object of the investigation was uranium 'monocarbide', nominally UC and for part of the study also uranium sesqui-carbide UC $_{1.5}$ $(\mathrm{U}_2\mathrm{C}_3)$ .
The temperature range of equilibrium and kinetics study was $550^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $970^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ .
The kinetics and equilibrium measurements were done for $1/4$ , $1/2$ , $1$ , $2$ , $4$ , $8$ up to 24 hours or longer.

The operations being sensitive against atmospheric were carried out in glove boxes under a controlled nitrogen atmosphere with approximately 10 ppm $\mathrm{O}_2$ and 10 ppm $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ .
In some cases the $\mathrm{O}_2$ concentration was a factor of 10 higher which could have influenced the results.

The criteria for the selection of the appropriate compounds for the postulated system represented by equation 1 are as follows: The desired free enthalpy change $\Delta G$ of this reaction should be as near to zero as possible in the temperature range $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C} - 900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ .
Not only will the value of the equilibrium constant $K_{\mathrm{eq}}$ be close to 1 but also the distribution ratio $D$ for uranium, where
D = \frac {\text {m o l a r f r a c t i o n o f U C l}}{\text {m o l a r f r a c t i o n o f U C}} 3
From a general point of view the electronegativity of any here chosen metal chloride should lie between a value of 1.2 (the electronegativity of uranium and plutonium) and 1.6.

The problem mentioned above (II,1) of the possible interaction of $\mathrm{UCl}_3$ in molten salt media with silica requires further study.
McIver (1966) writes: "The most obvious choice for a vessel is silica as this does not react with $\mathrm{UCl}_3$ .
Brown (1972) states that "uranium trichloride partially with silica at the temperature $850^{\circ}\mathrm{C} - 900^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the product being $\mathrm{UO}_2$ ."
In these experiments for dry substrates the reaction between $\mathrm{UCl}_3$ and $\mathrm{SiO}_2$ is very slow and only after 6-8 hours is the amount of $\mathrm{UO}_2$ formed significant.

Here we choose the method of solution in water at approx. $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for periods of 20 minutes.
Besson (1963) writes: "UC reacts readily with water only above $60^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Bradley (1962) has observed during the hydrolysis of UC in water at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 1 hour a very small gas evolution.
The time required to hydrolise a $3 - 4\mathrm{~g}$ irregularly shaped specimen at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was up to a week.
Robinson (1964) separated UC from Zn metal by solution in $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ .

From these it is clear that the effect of a 20 minute attack by water or by weak solution of hydrochloric acid (to prevent the hydrolysis of uranium compounds) at $10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ on the UC was practically negligible.
For a sample of solid uranium carbide of approx $0.5\mathrm{~g}$ of irregular shape in an arbitrary chosen ratio of uranium carbide to the metal chloride;

It must be stressed that the AlCl₃ acts in a different manner than the other metal chlorides and the reaction 4). and probably 5). may also occur, influencing the effective kinetics.

Robinson (1964) observed a non soluble residue in the system UC in $\mathrm{Zn}_{(\text{molten})} / \mathrm{ZnCl}_2$ (molten salt) in the form of a black uranium carbide phase in a zinc matrix without any uranium - zinc phase present but with $1.5\%$ oxygen.
The product of this reaction is uranium oxycarbide (equilibrium time some hours, temperature $\sim 600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).
The maximum fraction of the uranium in uranium carbide which could have been oxidized was 0.91.
A high concentration of $\mathrm{ZnCl}_2$ in the salt was necessary to accomplish this $91\%$ oxidation.

We need only say that $\mathrm{UCl}_3$ in this eutectic at $\sim 500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ has a positive deviation from ideal, the activity coefficient being of the order of 10 for low concentrations of $\mathrm{UCl}_3$ (for literature see Hardy 1966).
The activity of $\mathrm{UCl}_3$ in NaCl/KCl eutectic (Flenglas 1962) is reasonably close to that above.
The more exact measurements (Hardy 1966) of the system $\mathrm{UCl}_3 / \mathrm{NaCl}$ shows a positive deviation from ideal: the activity coefficient is essentially greater than 1.

The deviation is lower at lower concentrations but according to Kertes (1971) in all system studied ( $\mathrm{UCl}_3$ , $\mathrm{UCl}_4$ ).) the activity coefficients are reasonably constant over the concentration range - usually below 0.01 mol fraction.
Also the results of Krahe (1965) show a positive deviation for the $\mathrm{UCl}_3$ solid /LiCl - KCl system.
Harder, Long and Steinaway in their recent publication (1969) estimate the activity coefficient for $\mathrm{UCl}_3$ in NaCl as excess partial molar free enthalpy
for $0.3\mathrm{mol}$ fraction $\mathrm{UCl}_3$ in $\mathrm{NaCl} / \mathrm{UCl}_3$ .
Over the temperature range $600 - 800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the average value for the activity coefficient of $\mathrm{UCl}_3$ is therefore $0.25 \pm 0.10$ .
A sharp maximum at the melting point is highly indicative of the formation of a complex compound, a less pronounced maximum simply means a complex of lower thermodynamic stability (Kertes, 1971).
For the UCl₃ system it seems that a cautious examination of the phase diagrams points to the existence of two compounds, K₂UCl₅ and K₃UCl₆ (see Bagnall, 1969).
From this it can be concluded that the activity coefficient for UCl₃ is essentially lower than 1.
In addition PuCl₃ has an activity coefficient in the KCl medium lower than that for the NaCl system (Pascal 1960, p.
A) Partridge 1961 UCl/KCl

The system under investigation at equilibrium is a tertiary system (e.g. $\mathrm{UCl}_3 / \mathrm{MnCl}_2$ / MeCl where Me is an alkali metal) which may also be described as quasi-binary system $\mathrm{Me}_{\mathrm{x}}\mathrm{UCl}_{3} + \mathrm{x}$ $\mathrm{Me}_{\mathrm{y}}\mathrm{MnCl}_{2} + \mathrm{y}$ .

The case for $\mathrm{Mn}_{\mathrm{z}}\mathrm{UCl}_{3} + 2\mathrm{z}$ seems much less likely because the $\mathrm{Mn}^{+2}$ with a half filled d-electron shell and with a radius of $9\mathrm{nm}$ has the relation $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{MnCl}_2} / \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{Cl}} = 0.50$ which results in the octahedral co-ordination $\mathrm{MnCl}_6^{-3}$ . $\mathrm{U}^{+3}$ in a chloride medium has the same tendency for octahedral co-ordination: $\mathrm{UCl}_6^{-3}$ .
The systems of $\mathrm{MnCl}_2 / \mathrm{NaCl}$ , $\mathrm{MnCl}_2 / \mathrm{KCl}$ and $\mathrm{MnCl}_2/$ CsCl are investigated by Seifert (1966).
